criteria: The agent has read the abstract and introduction to understand that the paper compares vanilla Transformer, modified (recurrence-free) Transformer, and LSTM for rainfall-runoff prediction on the CAMELS benchmark dataset.

The agent has read the Results and Discussion section to understand the experimental setup: single-forcing on 671 basins, multi-forcing on 531 basins, training from October 1, 1999 to September 30, 2008, and testing from October 1, 1989 to September 30, 1999.

The agent has read Table 1 to understand the comparative performance metrics (KGE mean, FHV mean, FLV mean, and their standard deviations) for LSTM, vanilla Transformer, and modified Transformer under both single-forcing (NLDAS) and multi-forcing conditions.

The agent has read Figure 1 to understand the four CDF comparisons (NSE, KGE, FLV, FHV) across LSTM, vanilla Transformer, modified Transformer, and SAC-SMA models for both single and multi-forcing configurations.

The agent has read the introduction to understand the three hypotheses offered for the vanilla Transformer's underperformance: data size limitation, Markovian nature of hydrologic processes, and lack of memory for long-term effects.

The agent has read the Results and Discussion section to understand that the modified Transformer achieves KGE 0.74 (single-forcing) and 0.80 (multi-forcing), matching LSTM, while the vanilla Transformer scores 0.71 and 0.77 respectively.
The agent has read the introduction to understand that Kratzert et al. 2019 established the CAMELS benchmark with KGE 0.72 on 531 basins with NLDAS alone, and KGE 0.80 with multiple forcing datasets.
The plan includes loading and preprocessing the CAMELS dataset, including both meteorological forcing time series and static catchment attributes for each basin.
The plan specifies selecting 671 basins for single-forcing experiments and a subset of 531 basins for multi-forcing experiments.
The plan specifies the training period from October 1, 1999 to September 30, 2008 and the testing period from October 1, 1989 to September 30, 1999.
The plan includes implementing the LSTM baseline model following the architecture of Kratzert et al. 2019.
The plan includes implementing the vanilla Transformer encoder architecture for daily streamflow prediction.
The plan includes implementing the modified recurrence-free Transformer architecture as described in the paper.
The plan includes using NLDAS as the single forcing dataset and merging NLDAS, Maurer, and Daymet datasets for the multi-forcing experiments.
The plan includes training 10 ensemble members with different random seeds for each model configuration.
The plan includes implementing all four evaluation metrics: NSE, KGE, FHV (high-flow bias), and FLV (low-flow bias).
The plan includes computing ensemble mean and standard deviation of all performance metrics across the 10 trained models.
The plan includes generating CDF (Cumulative Density Function) plots of NSE, KGE, FHV, and FLV across all basins for each model variant.

The code for loading and preprocessing the CAMELS dataset, including time-series meteorological forcings (precipitation, temperature, etc.) and static catchment attributes (e.g., area, mean elevation, aridity), has been implemented.
The code for splitting the CAMELS basins into the correct sets (671 for single-forcing, 531 for multi-forcing) has been implemented.
The code correctly defines training data from October 1, 1999 to September 30, 2008 and test data from October 1, 1989 to September 30, 1999 for all model experiments.
The code for loading and aligning the NLDAS forcing dataset for the single-forcing experiments has been implemented.
The code for loading and merging multiple forcing datasets (NLDAS, Maurer, Daymet) as model inputs for the multi-forcing experiments has been implemented.
The LSTM model following Kratzert et al. 2019 (with LSTM hidden states and static catchment attribute embedding) has been implemented as the baseline.
The vanilla Transformer encoder architecture for sequential hydrologic prediction has been implemented, using the standard attention mechanism from Vaswani et al. 2017.
The modified recurrence-free Transformer architecture (the proposed variant that outperforms the vanilla Transformer) has been implemented.
The code for incorporating static catchment attributes as additional inputs to both the Transformer and LSTM models has been implemented.
The training loop with mini-batch gradient descent for both LSTM and Transformer models has been implemented, supporting the 10-seed ensemble setup.
The code for training 10 ensemble members with distinct random seeds, each producing an independent model, has been implemented.
The Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (NSE) metric has been implemented to evaluate daily streamflow predictions at the basin level.
The Kling-Gupta Efficiency (KGE) metric has been implemented to evaluate daily streamflow predictions at the basin level.
The Flow High Variability (FHV) metric quantifying high-flow bias (typically computed over the top 2% of flow values) has been implemented.
The Flow Low Variability (FLV) metric quantifying low-flow bias (typically computed over the bottom 30% of flow values) has been implemented.
The code for aggregating per-basin metrics into ensemble mean and standard deviation across the 10 model members has been implemented.
The code for generating CDF plots of NSE, KGE, FLV, and FHV across all basins for LSTM, vanilla Transformer, modified Transformer, and SAC-SMA has been implemented.
The code for the SAC-SMA (Sacramento Soil Moisture Accounting) model results or their loading from reference data has been implemented to appear as a baseline in Figure 1.
The LSTM model has been retrained on 671 basins with single NLDAS forcing as described in Table 1 (not just using the 531-basin results from Kratzert et al. 2019).

The code for the single-forcing (NLDAS) experiments has been executed successfully for all three model types (LSTM, vanilla Transformer, modified Transformer) on 671 basins, producing 10 ensemble members each.
The code for the multi-forcing experiments (NLDAS + Maurer + Daymet) has been executed successfully for all three model types on 531 basins, producing 10 ensemble members each.
The evaluation metrics (NSE, KGE, FHV, FLV) have been computed successfully for all model variants on the test period (October 1989 to September 1999) without errors.
The CDF plots (Figure 1) comparing NSE, KGE, FLV, and FHV for all model variants have been generated and saved without errors.
The ensemble statistics (mean and standard deviation of each metric) have been computed and reported for all six model configurations (3 models × 2 forcing setups).
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the LSTM single-forcing (NLDAS) KGE mean is approximately 0.73 ± 0.003.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the vanilla Transformer single-forcing KGE mean is approximately 0.71 ± 0.007, confirming it underperforms LSTM.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the modified Transformer single-forcing KGE mean is approximately 0.74 ± 0.007, slightly exceeding LSTM.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the LSTM multi-forcing KGE mean is approximately 0.80 ± 0.004.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the modified Transformer multi-forcing KGE mean is approximately 0.80 ± 0.007, matching LSTM.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: the vanilla Transformer single-forcing FHV mean is approximately -26.66 ± 2.83, substantially worse than LSTM's -17.49 ± 0.58, showing the vanilla Transformer's weakness for high-flow events.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the modified Transformer single-forcing FHV mean is approximately -18.00 ± 2.94, comparable to LSTM's -17.49 ± 0.58.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the modified Transformer multi-forcing FHV mean is approximately -9.19 ± 4.01, slightly better than LSTM's -11.91 ± 0.549.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: the LSTM single-forcing FLV mean is approximately -2.82 ± 8.15, while the modified Transformer FLV is 2.28 ± 4.24, showing the modified Transformer has lower variability in low-flow performance.

The execution outcomes confirm Figure 1: the CDF curves show the modified Transformer and LSTM achieving nearly identical NSE and KGE distributions, while the vanilla Transformer's curve is shifted to the left (lower performance).

The execution outcomes confirm Figure 1: the CDF for FHV shows the vanilla Transformer biased high (positive FHV values) more frequently than LSTM and modified Transformer, indicating systematic high-flow overestimation or underestimation.

The execution outcomes confirm the key finding that the modified Transformer ensemble standard deviation of KGE (0.007) is larger than that of LSTM (0.003), indicating greater stochastic variability between seeds for the Transformer.

The execution outcomes confirm that multi-forcing improves performance for all three model types over single-forcing (e.g., KGE increases from 0.73 to 0.80 for LSTM, from 0.71 to 0.77 for vanilla Transformer, and from 0.74 to 0.80 for the modified Transformer).
The execution outcomes confirm that the vanilla Transformer multi-forcing KGE is 0.77 ± 0.016, with a notably larger standard deviation compared to LSTM (0.004) and modified Transformer (0.007).
